questions.specification:
A smart city initiative uses federated learning to train machine learning models across multiple hospitals, schools, and government agencies while preserving data privacy.

**Paper/Level note (metadata correction required):** This question targets HL-only machine learning content and is appropriate for Paper 2 structured response rather than Paper 1 MCQ.

parts.content: Explain the federated learning process and how it enables collaborative machine learning without centralizing sensitive data.
parts.markscheme:
- Local institutions download the current global model and train it locally on their private datasets. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Only model updates (for example, parameter changes/gradients) are sent back to a central server, so raw data never leaves the local source. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The central server aggregates the received updates (for example, using Federated Averaging) to produce an improved global model without accessing sensitive raw data. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline challenges and limitations of federated learning in real-world smart city deployments.
parts.markscheme:
- Communication overhead/high network latency can slow synchronisation between geographically distributed institutions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Data heterogeneity (non-IID data) across hospitals/schools can hinder convergence or reduce accuracy. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A computer vision system uses convolutional neural networks for medical image analysis including X-rays, MRIs, and CT scans for diagnostic assistance.

parts.content: Explain how convolutional layers, pooling layers, and fully connected layers work together in CNN architectures for medical image classification.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that convolutional layers apply learnable filters to extract local features such as edges or anatomical structures from medical images. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe how these filters create feature maps that identify specific spatial patterns relevant for diagnosis. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that pooling layers reduce the spatial dimensions of these feature maps to decrease computational complexity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that pooling provides spatial invariance, allowing the network to recognize features despite small translations or distortions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe how fully connected layers aggregate the extracted local features to perform global reasoning on the entire image. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that the final fully connected layer uses these features to output classification probabilities for specific diagnostic categories. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe the challenges of training CNNs on medical images and explain why transfer learning is particularly valuable in healthcare applications.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe challenges such as limited availability of high-quality labelled datasets due to patient privacy regulations and the high cost of expert annotation by radiologists. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that transfer learning is valuable because it leverages pre-trained weights from large-scale general datasets, allowing high performance even with small medical-specific datasets. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A healthcare AI system uses ensemble methods and deep learning to analyse medical images for cancer detection and treatment planning. The following table summarises various ensemble techniques used in medical AI models.

*In this table, “Uncertainty quantification” refers to the model’s capability to provide reliable confidence/uncertainty estimates (e.g., through agreement/disagreement across models), not the amount of uncertainty in any single prediction.*

| Ensemble Method | Combination Strategy | Diversity Source | Medical Application | Uncertainty Quantification Capability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bagging | Average/aggregate predictions (e.g., mean/majority vote) | Bootstrap sampling | Reduce overfitting in image analysis / improve medical imaging accuracy | Medium |
| Boosting | Sequential learning (add models to correct previous errors) | Focus on difficult (misclassified) cases | Rare disease detection | Low |
| Stacking | Meta-model (second-level learner combines base models) | Different algorithms | Complex diagnosis tasks / multi-modal medical analysis | High |
| Voting | Majority vote (hard voting) | Different models/initialisations/training subsets | Binary classification (e.g., benign vs malignant) | Medium |

parts.content: Discuss how uncertainty quantification in medical AI models helps clinicians make informed decisions.
parts.markscheme:
- Confidence scores help identify cases requiring human expert review <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uncertainty estimates guide additional testing or imaging recommendations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Risk assessment for treatment decisions based on model confidence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Quality control by flagging low-confidence predictions <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the ensemble learning techniques listed in the table and their advantages for medical diagnosis applications. Your response should address combination strategies, sources of diversity, and uncertainty quantification.
parts.markscheme:
- **Bagging**: Combination Strategy: Average predictions/Bootstrap aggregation/Combine multiple models; Medical Application: Reduce overfitting in image analysis/Improve medical imaging accuracy; Uncertainty Quantification: Medium/Moderate capability to quantify uncertainty <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Boosting**: Diversity Source: Focus on difficult cases/Hard examples/Misclassified samples; Uncertainty Quantification: Low/Limited capability to quantify uncertainty <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Stacking**: Combination Strategy: Meta-learner/Second-level model/Model of models; Diversity Source: Different model types/Various algorithms/Multiple approaches; Medical Application: Complex diagnosis tasks/Multi-modal medical analysis; Uncertainty Quantification: High/Strong capability to quantify uncertainty <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Voting**: Combination Strategy: Majority vote/Democratic decision/Simple voting; Medical Application: Binary classification tasks/Yes-no decisions/Benign vs malignant classification; Uncertainty Quantification: Medium/Vote-based confidence/Moderate capability to quantify uncertainty <marks>2 marks</marks>

questions.specification: A cybersecurity system employs various machine learning techniques including support vector machines, random forests, and neural networks for threat detection and malware classification.

parts.content:
Complete the following table to outline a multi-algorithm approach for cybersecurity threat detection:

| Algorithm Type | Input Features | Detection Capability | False Positive Rate | Computational Overhead | Adversarial Robustness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Support Vector Machines | - | Binary classification | - | - | - |
| Random Forest | - | - | Low | Medium | - |
| Neural Networks | Raw network traffic | High | - | - | - |
| Ensemble Methods | - | - | - | - | High |
parts.markscheme:
- **Support Vector Machines**:
  - Input Features: statistical network features or engineered security features <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - False Positive Rate: Medium/Moderate <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Computational Overhead: Medium/Moderate <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Adversarial Robustness: Medium resistance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Random Forest**:
  - Input Features: mixed feature types (categorical and numerical) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Detection Capability: multi-class threat classification or complex pattern recognition <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Adversarial Robustness: High/Strong resistance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Neural Networks**:
  - False Positive Rate: Variable (depends on architecture and training) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Computational Overhead: High (significant training and inference resources required) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Adversarial Robustness: Low (vulnerable to adversarial perturbations) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Ensemble Methods**:
  - Input Features: combined outputs from individual models or multi-modal features <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Detection Capability: comprehensive threat coverage/Hybrid detection <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - False Positive Rate: optimized (lower than individual algorithms) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Computational Overhead: High (requires execution of multiple models) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyse how adversarial attacks specifically target machine learning security systems and describe two defence strategies.
parts.markscheme:
- Adversarial examples: inputs intentionally designed to cause a model to make a mistake by adding small, often imperceptible, perturbations that shift the data point across a decision boundary <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evasion attacks: modifying malicious samples (such as malware code or network packets) during the inference phase so they are misclassified as benign by the security system <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Defence strategy 1: adversarial training, which involves including known adversarial examples in the training dataset to increase the model's resilience <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Defence strategy 2: implementing multiple validation layers, input sanitisation, or using diverse ensembles to reduce the success rate of attacks targeting a single model's architecture <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A financial services company implements basic machine learning for algorithmic trading and portfolio optimization in dynamic market environments.

parts.content: Compare different machine learning approaches for financial applications, considering sample efficiency, stability, and interpretability.
parts.markscheme:
- Compare a supervised learning approach (e.g. regression/classification) by noting it can be relatively sample efficient when labelled historical data is available and can be stable at inference time, but it may struggle with non-stationary markets and depends on the quality/representativeness of training data. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compare decision trees / random forests by noting they can provide more interpretability (feature importance / rule-like decisions) and can model non-linear relationships, but may overfit without careful validation and may be less stable across changing market regimes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compare simple neural networks by noting they can model complex patterns and interactions, but are typically less interpretable and may require more data/tuning to achieve stable generalisation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compare unsupervised learning (e.g. clustering) by noting it can be used without labelled outcomes to group assets/market conditions and support diversification, but results can be sensitive to feature scaling/choices and clusters may change over time (stability). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Make an explicit comparison against at least one other approach on at least one of: sample efficiency, stability, interpretability (must be comparative, not just descriptive). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall judgement that links the comparison back to financial use (trading/portfolio decisions in dynamic markets). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the trade-off between adapting a model to new data and relying on the current model applies to algorithmic trading and describe strategies to balance this trade-off.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain “adapting to new data” as updating or changing a model/strategy in response to new market information to potentially improve future performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain “relying on the current model” as continuing to use the existing best-performing model/strategy to maintain consistent performance and avoid unnecessary changes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe a strategy such as a rolling/expanding training window with regular retraining (or scheduled updates) to incorporate new data while controlling how much history is kept. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe a strategy such as validation/early stopping, monitoring drift with performance thresholds, or using an ensemble to reduce instability when updating. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A search engine company uses machine learning for query understanding, document ranking, and personalized search results across billions of web pages.

parts.content: Explain how different machine learning approaches contribute to modern search engine functionality, including natural language processing and ranking algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>2</marks> for each of the following areas:

*   **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**:
    *   Query understanding and intent recognition (e.g., determining if a user is looking for a product or information) <marks>A1</marks>
    *   Semantic search allows for matching concepts rather than just keywords, improving the depth of results <marks>A1</marks>

*   **Ranking Algorithms**:
    *   Learning-to-rank (LTR) algorithms optimize the order of results based on historical relevance scoring <marks>A1</marks>
    *   Click-through models evaluate the effectiveness of rankings by monitoring user selection patterns <marks>A1</marks>

*   **Personalization**:
    *   User behaviour modeling and preference learning to tailor results to individual history <marks>A1</marks>
    *   Context awareness (e.g., location, device type, or time of day) to provide more relevant local or timely results <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 6 marks]

parts.content: Describe how online learning enables search engines to adapt quickly to changing user behaviour and emerging topics.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>A1</marks> for each of the following points, up to <marks>4</marks> marks:

*   Utilizes real-time feedback from user clicks and interactions to adjust weights immediately <marks>A1</marks>
*   Enables incremental model updates, allowing the system to learn from new data without the need for a full, computationally expensive retraining cycle <marks>A1</marks>
*   Allows for rapid adaptation to trending topics, breaking news, and seasonal queries that would be missed by static models <marks>A1</marks>
*   Integrates with A/B testing frameworks, allowing for the continuous evaluation and deployment of slightly modified algorithms in a live environment <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 4 marks]

questions.specification: A recommendation system uses collaborative filtering techniques to suggest products to customers based on purchase history and user similarities.

parts.content: Compare collaborative filtering approaches (user-based vs item-based) and discuss their computational complexity and recommendation quality.
parts.markscheme:
- User-based collaborative filtering identifies users with similar patterns to recommend items; it can provide diverse suggestions but has a high computational complexity of $O(n^2)$ where $n$ is the number of users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Item-based collaborative filtering calculates similarity between items based on user ratings; it is generally more stable and computationally efficient if the number of items $m$ is smaller than the number of users $n$, with complexity $O(m^2)$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recommendation quality for both methods depends significantly on data sparsity; systems struggle to find meaningful similarities when the user-item interaction matrix is mostly empty. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clear comparison of how recommendations are produced (user-user vs item-item similarity) and/or typical trade-offs in quality (e.g., diversity vs stability). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct discussion of relative computational costs (scaling with users vs scaling with items) linked to which dimension is larger. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Appropriate discussion of factors affecting quality (e.g., sparsity, noisy data) applied to both methods. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the cold start problem affects recommendation systems and describe solutions to address this challenge.
parts.markscheme:
- The cold start problem occurs when new users or items lack sufficient historical interaction data, making it difficult for collaborative filtering algorithms to generate accurate recommendations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains the impact (e.g., poor/irrelevant recommendations until enough interactions are collected). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solution: use content-based filtering (utilizing item metadata) to recommend for new users/items. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solution: collect demographic/preferences during user registration and/or use popularity-based defaults for new entries. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A manufacturing company uses data analysis to identify production defects and optimize quality control processes.

parts.content: Compare different data analysis methods for manufacturing quality control, considering data types, scalability, and interpretability requirements.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe how summary statistics and simple thresholds can be used to compare batches/production runs for numeric sensor data, and are computationally efficient and scalable <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how control charts (e.g., Shewhart charts) support interpretable monitoring of process variation over time and can highlight when a process is out of control <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outline how stratifying/segmenting data by categorical variables (e.g., machine ID, shift, supplier) supports interpretation of defect rates and can reveal patterns by group <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe how simple visualizations (e.g., histograms, box plots, line graphs) help interpret distributions and trends in quality measurements <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain scalability considerations: methods based on single-pass/streaming calculations (means, counts, moving averages) scale better than approaches requiring repeated full-dataset comparisons <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compare suitability by data type: numeric time-series data suits trend/control-chart analysis, while categorical defect codes suit frequency tables/cross-tabulation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how quality monitoring techniques can identify equipment failures and quality defects in manufacturing processes.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe statistical monitoring methods such as control charts and rule/threshold-based alerts used for tracking sensor readings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how trends such as increasing variance, drift in mean, or repeated threshold breaches can indicate equipment degradation or imminent failure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how time-series analysis ideas such as moving averages/rate-of-change can be used to detect unusual operational patterns <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State how real-time monitoring enables immediate intervention (e.g., stopping a line, recalibrating equipment, quarantining a batch) to reduce defects <marks>1 mark</marks>
